h1Title: Amnestic Disorder: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Amnestic disorder due to a known physiological condition (ICD-10: F04) is a persistent impairment of learning new information or recalling previously known material caused by an identifiable brain injury or disease — not by substance use alone or a broader dementia syndrome. The most common cause globally is thiamine deficiency in chronic alcohol use disorder, producing the Korsakoff amnestic syndrome; vascular, traumatic, infectious, and hypoxic injuries account for most remaining cases. Patients retain procedural memory and general intelligence while displaying striking anterograde amnesia and variable retrograde loss. Without treatment of the underlying cause, the syndrome tends to be permanent in roughly 75% of Korsakoff cases. Early thiamine replacement and rehabilitation are the primary interventions.

keyStats.prevalence: Korsakoff syndrome, the most common form, affects an estimated 1-2% of the general population in high-income countries and up to 12-14% of chronic heavy drinkers (Arts et al. 2017)
keyStats.demographics: Men slightly outnumber women (approximately 2:1) in alcohol-related cases; traumatic brain injury cases affect all ages but peak in males aged 15-35
keyStats.avgAge: Korsakoff syndrome onset typically age 40-70 in alcohol-dependent patients; TBI-related amnestic disorder can occur at any age
keyStats.globalCases: No reliable global case count; Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome is estimated to cause 0.5-2% of all dementia-like presentations in hospital neurology services worldwide

definition: Amnestic disorder due to a known physiological condition (ICD-10: F04) is a syndrome of significant memory impairment — disproportionate to any other cognitive deficits present — directly caused by a specific, identifiable medical condition affecting brain structure or function. The diagnosis requires evidence from history, physical examination, or investigations that the memory disturbance is a direct physiological consequence of the causal condition, and that it does not occur exclusively during delirium, does not meet criteria for dementia, and is not better explained by a dissociative disorder or substance intoxication alone. Memory impairment is bilateral in most cases, involving dysfunction of the hippocampus, fornix, mammillary bodies, or medial diencephalon — the circuit that consolidates new declarative memories. The condition splits into anterograde amnesia, the inability to lay down new memories after the insult, and retrograde amnesia, loss of information stored before the insult; most physiological amnestic disorders produce more anterograde than retrograde impairment. Neuropsychiatric features such as confabulation, apathy, and temporal disorientation accompany the core memory deficit, particularly in Korsakoff syndrome. Managed by neurologists, neuropsychologists, and in alcohol-related cases by addiction medicine physicians, the syndrome requires a combination of treating the causal condition and intensive cognitive rehabilitation.

typesClassification.description: Results from thiamine deficiency damaging the mammillary bodies and medial thalamus, almost always following Wernicke encephalopathy. Characterized by profound anterograde amnesia, retrograde amnesia spanning years to decades, confabulation, and preserved procedural memory. Without thiamine repletion, 80% of cases become permanent.

Follows moderate-to-severe traumatic brain injury affecting hippocampal and parahippocampal structures. Post-traumatic amnesia duration (from injury to continuous day-to-day memory) is the best predictor of long-term outcome; duration under 1 hour predicts good recovery, over 4 weeks predicts severe disability.

Hippocampal CA1 neurons are selectively vulnerable to oxygen deprivation. Cardiac arrest, near-drowning, or severe respiratory failure lasting 3-10 minutes produces selective anterograde amnesia without other major deficits in survivors.

Herpes simplex virus type 1 has a predilection for temporal lobes, producing severe bilateral anterograde amnesia and variable retrograde loss even after antiviral treatment. Lasting memory impairment occurs in 50-70% of survivors.

Posterior cerebral artery or thalamic infarcts can produce acute amnesia by damaging the hippocampus or thalamus bilaterally. Bilateral hippocampal stroke is rare; isolated thalamic infarcts account for 5-10% of stroke-related amnesias.

primarySymptoms: Anterograde amnesia — the inability to retain new information beyond seconds to minutes, tested by asking patients to recall three words after a 5-minute delay; severe cases recall zero items even with cues.

Retrograde amnesia — loss of autobiographical and semantic memories from weeks, months, or years before the brain insult, with a temporal gradient (most recent memories lost first) in most vascular and traumatic cases.
Confabulation — the spontaneous production of false memories without intent to deceive, often plausible and detailed, particularly prominent in Korsakoff syndrome and orbitofrontal injury.
Temporal disorientation — inability to state the current date, month, or year, often wildly inaccurate, despite knowing one's name and basic personal details.
Impaired episodic memory with preserved procedural and semantic memory — patients can learn motor skills, priming tasks, and retain general knowledge but cannot recall learning any of it.
Lack of insight into memory deficits — many patients deny or minimize their memory impairment, leading to resistance to rehabilitation and unsafe self-management.
Apathy and emotional blunting — reduced spontaneous initiative, flat affect, and diminished emotional responsiveness, most prominent in thiamine-deficiency and frontal-injury cases.

Verbal and visual memory loss — both modalities affected, though lesion laterality can produce relative asymmetry (left-sided lesions impairing verbal memory more, right-sided impairing visuospatial recall).
Repetitive questioning — patients repeatedly ask the same question within minutes, unaware they have already asked it, causing significant distress to caregivers.
earlyWarningSigns: Acute confusional state or Wernicke triad (ophthalmoplegia, ataxia, confusion) in a patient with alcohol use disorder or malnutrition — thiamine must be given immediately before the amnestic phase sets in.
Post-traumatic amnesia persisting beyond 24 hours after a head injury — indicates moderate-to-severe TBI and warrants inpatient neurological monitoring.
Abrupt onset of memory loss without explanation in the context of new fever, headache, and temporal-lobe signal change on MRI — herpes encephalitis requires urgent diagnosis and acyclovir.
Persistent day-to-day memory complaints more than 3 months after a stroke, particularly a posterior cerebral artery territory event — dedicated neuropsychological testing is indicated.
emergencySigns: Wernicke triad (nystagmus or ophthalmoplegia, ataxia, acute confusional state) in any malnourished or alcohol-dependent patient — intravenous thiamine 500 mg three times daily must be given before glucose and before waiting for serum thiamine results.
Fever, acute behavioral change, and temporal-lobe abnormality on MRI or CSF pleocytosis — empirical intravenous acyclovir for suspected herpes encephalitis while awaiting HSV PCR.
Sudden dense anterograde amnesia lasting hours with complete recovery in an older patient — transient global amnesia (TGA), benign but requires urgent vascular workup to exclude TIA.
Post-cardiac-arrest survivor with isolated dense anterograde amnesia on ICU awakening — suggests selective hippocampal anoxic injury; neurology review needed urgently.

causes.cause: Thiamine (vitamin B1) deficiency from chronic alcohol use
causes.description: Chronic heavy alcohol consumption reduces dietary thiamine intake and impairs intestinal absorption. Thiamine depletion depletes transketolase activity in neurons of the mammillary bodies and thalamus within days, producing Wernicke encephalopathy; untreated, 80% progress to the permanent Korsakoff amnestic syndrome characterized by profound anterograde amnesia and confabulation.

Coup-contrecoup acceleration-deceleration forces shear axons throughout the hippocampus and fornices, while direct contusions frequently impact temporal and frontal lobes. Bilateral hippocampal damage produces lasting anterograde amnesia proportional to the duration of post-traumatic amnesia — the best validated predictor of memory outcome after TBI.

HSV-1 spreads from the trigeminal ganglion to the temporal lobes via direct neural pathways, producing necrotizing inflammation that destroys hippocampal CA1 neurons and parahippocampal gyri bilaterally. Even with timely acyclovir treatment, 50-70% of survivors retain significant amnestic deficits, often of severe degree.

Global cerebral hypoxia lasting 3-10 minutes from cardiac arrest, respiratory failure, or near-drowning selectively destroys CA1 hippocampal neurons while sparing cortex. Survivors who regain consciousness often display a striking amnestic syndrome with preserved intelligence, language, and personality but virtually no ability to form new episodic memories.

Posterior cerebral artery infarcts can damage both hippocampi; paramedian thalamic artery occlusion, which supplies the medial thalamus bilaterally from a single vessel variant present in up to 4% of the population, produces acute bilateral thalamic injury and a permanent dense amnesia with preserved motor function.

Severe folate deficiency (pellagra niacin deficiency affects memory through diffuse cortical involvement), carbon monoxide poisoning (selective putaminal and hippocampal damage), and chemotherapy-associated cognitive impairment all produce physiologically mediated amnestic syndromes. Thiamine deficiency from hyperemesis gravidarum, bariatric surgery, or cancer-related malnutrition follows the same Wernicke-Korsakoff pathway as alcohol-related cases.

riskFactors.description: The single largest risk factor globally. Chronic heavy drinkers have 12-14% lifetime prevalence of Korsakoff syndrome (Arts et al. 2017). Combined inadequate diet and impaired thiamine absorption are the proximate mechanism.

Thiamine stores last only 18-21 days. Bariatric surgery, severe hyperemesis gravidarum, cancer-related anorexia, and prolonged parenteral nutrition without vitamin supplementation all deplete thiamine rapidly.

TBI with loss of consciousness over 30 minutes or post-traumatic amnesia over 24 hours reliably produces some degree of lasting anterograde amnesia. Falls in older adults and road-traffic accidents in young men are the leading mechanisms.

riskFactors.factor: History of Wernicke encephalopathy without thiamine treatment
riskFactors.description: Wernicke encephalopathy treated promptly with high-dose IV thiamine converts to full recovery in roughly 80% of cases; left untreated or undertreated, 80% develop the permanent Korsakoff amnestic syndrome.

riskFactors.factor: Herpes simplex virus exposure and immunosuppression
riskFactors.description: HSV-1 seropositive individuals — the majority of the adult population — are at risk of reactivation encephalitis, particularly in the context of immunosuppression from HIV, transplant medications, or aging.

riskFactors.factor: Cardiovascular disease and cardiac arrest history
riskFactors.description: Cardiac arrest with return of spontaneous circulation carries a 20-40% rate of significant hippocampal injury detectable on MRI; risk correlates directly with duration of no-flow or low-flow period before resuscitation.
Hippocampal neuronal reserve declines with age, making older adults more vulnerable to permanent amnesia after the same insult compared with younger patients.

affectedDemographics: Men with alcohol use disorder aged 40-70 account for the majority of Korsakoff syndrome cases; prevalence among alcohol-dependent men is approximately 12%, twice that in alcohol-dependent women in hospital-based series (Arts et al. 2017).
Young adults aged 15-35 sustain the most severe traumatic brain injuries through road-traffic accidents; memory impairment is among the most common and disabling residual deficits.
Older adults aged 65+ are overrepresented in cardiac-arrest survivors with hippocampal injury and in posterior-circulation stroke with amnesia.
Immunocompromised individuals — HIV-positive patients, transplant recipients, patients on high-dose steroids — face elevated risk of HSV reactivation encephalitis and consequent permanent amnesia.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis requires identifying the causal physiological condition and documenting the pattern of memory impairment through formal neuropsychological testing. A thorough history focuses on alcohol and nutritional intake, head trauma, cardiac events, viral illnesses, and the temporal relationship between insult and symptom onset. Clinical examination assesses level of consciousness, ocular motility, coordination, and preliminary bedside cognition with the Montreal Cognitive Assessment or Mini-Mental State Examination; however, these tools are insensitive for isolated amnestic disorders — formal neuropsychological testing with delayed recall, recognition memory, and procedural memory batteries is required to characterize the pattern and severity. Neuroimaging is mandatory: MRI brain with T2-weighted fluid-attenuated inversion recovery (FLAIR) sequences identifies hippocampal atrophy or signal change, mammillary body atrophy (characteristic of Korsakoff), thalamic lesions, encephalitic signal, or old infarcts. In suspected Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome, serum thiamine and erythrocyte transketolase activity are measured, though treatment must not wait for results. Cerebrospinal fluid HSV PCR is ordered when encephalitis is possible. Electroencephalography (EEG) is performed if seizures complicate the presentation. Functional neuroimaging such as FDG-PET can confirm bilateral hippocampal hypometabolism in ambiguous cases. The differential diagnosis includes delirium, dementia, dissociative amnesia (F44.0), transient global amnesia (G45.4), severe depression with pseudodementia, and complex partial seizures.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Formally characterizes anterograde and retrograde amnesia, semantic vs episodic memory, implicit vs explicit learning, and confabulation tendency
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 1-3 hour session with a neuropsychologist; involves word-list learning, story recall after a delay, visuospatial reproduction, and recognition tests. Results return within days as a written report.

diagnosticTests.test: MRI brain with FLAIR, DWI, and coronal hippocampal sequences
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies structural cause: hippocampal atrophy/signal change, mammillary body atrophy, thalamic lesions, encephalitic change, vascular lesions
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 30-45 minutes lying still in the scanner; no injection required unless contrast enhancement is needed for encephalitis. Coronal FLAIR is essential for hippocampal morphology assessment.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms or raises suspicion for thiamine deficiency in alcohol-related or nutritional amnestic disorder
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; results in 1-3 days. Low thiamine activity supports the diagnosis, but treatment must begin empirically before results return in any at-risk patient.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Diagnoses herpes encephalitis in patients with fever, behavioral change, and temporal-lobe MRI changes
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A lumbar puncture under local anaesthetic in the lower back, taking about 15-20 minutes. The specimen is sent for cell count, glucose, protein, and viral PCR panel; HSV result usually returns within 24-48 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Initial screening for memory and attention domains; aids in identifying cases requiring formal neuropsychological referral
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 10-minute paper-based test of orientation, drawing, memory recall, and attention administered by a clinician. Scores below 26/30 prompt specialist referral; isolated amnesia may produce subtle reductions on recall items only.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes non-convulsive status epilepticus or complex partial seizures mimicking or complicating amnestic disorder, particularly in encephalitis cases
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Small disc electrodes attached to the scalp; the test takes 30-60 minutes and involves staying still, then hyperventilating briefly and viewing flashing lights. No pain or electricity involved.

treatmentOverview: Treatment begins with aggressively addressing the underlying cause, as recovery depends almost entirely on how quickly and completely the brain insult is reversed. In Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome, the Royal College of Physicians 2014 guidelines and EFNS 2010 consensus recommend intravenous thiamine 500 mg in 100 mL normal saline over 30 minutes three times daily for 3-5 days, never substituting oral thiamine for the initial parenteral course; oral B-complex vitamins follow for weeks to months. Glucose must not be given before thiamine in a malnourished or alcohol-dependent patient, as it can precipitate acute Wernicke encephalopathy by depleting residual thiamine stores. For herpes encephalitis, intravenous acyclovir 10 mg/kg every 8 hours for 14-21 days is standard; earlier initiation correlates directly with better memory outcomes. Traumatic brain injury management follows Brain Trauma Foundation protocols (intracranial pressure control, haemodynamic optimisation, early rehabilitation); post-traumatic amnesia itself requires no specific pharmacological treatment but does predict outcome severity. Post-anoxic amnesia has no specific reversal therapy; neuroprotective cooling protocols post-cardiac arrest reduce hippocampal injury when initiated within hours. Rehabilitation is central for all forms. Neuropsychological rehabilitation focusing on errorless learning techniques, spaced-retrieval practice, and environmental adaptation (memory books, smartphones, structured routines) substantially improves functional independence even when the underlying amnesia cannot be reversed. Cholinesterase inhibitors (donepezil, rivastigmine) have modest supportive evidence in post-traumatic and post-encephalitic amnesia in small trials; they are not licensed specifically for amnestic disorder but used off-label by specialist services. Behavioural management for confabulation includes consistent orienting environments and calendars rather than confrontation.

medicalTreatments.name: IV thiamine (500 mg three times daily for 3-5 days, then oral)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line emergency treatment for Wernicke encephalopathy and Korsakoff syndrome. Repletes depleted transketolase-dependent pathways in diencephalon and hippocampus. Must precede glucose administration in any malnourished or alcohol-dependent patient.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Prompt IV thiamine prevents progression to permanent Korsakoff syndrome in approximately 80% of Wernicke cases; delays in treatment after Wernicke presentation reduce response rates substantially (Thomson et al. 2012)

medicalTreatments.name: IV acyclovir 10 mg/kg every 8 hours for 14-21 days
medicalTreatments.description: Antiviral treatment for herpes simplex encephalitis. Inhibits HSV DNA polymerase; earlier initiation is associated with better cognitive and memory outcomes. Renal function monitoring required during treatment.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces mortality from 70% (untreated) to under 20%; reduces severe disability including profound amnesia, though 50-70% of survivors still have lasting memory impairment (Skoldenberg et al. PMID 2908244)

medicalTreatments.name: Donepezil (5-10 mg daily) or rivastigmine (3-12 mg daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Cholinesterase inhibitors used off-label in post-traumatic and post-encephalitic amnestic disorders to enhance cholinergic neurotransmission in surviving memory circuits. Evidence is limited to small trials.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Modest improvement in verbal memory scores in post-TBI trials (NNT approximately 5-7 for clinically meaningful benefit); largest benefit seen within 12 months of injury (Zhang et al. 2014)

medicalTreatments.name: Multidisciplinary neuropsychological rehabilitation (errorless learning, spaced retrieval, memory aids)
medicalTreatments.description: Structured approach where patients practice tasks without making errors (errorless learning) and receive rehearsal at expanding intervals (spaced retrieval). Supplemented by external memory aids: notebooks, smartphones, whiteboards, structured daily schedules.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cochrane reviews confirm functional improvement in daily living and reduced caregiver burden in amnestic disorders regardless of underlying cause; acquisition of procedural skills preserved even with dense anterograde amnesia

medicalTreatments.name: Alcohol abstinence and nutritional rehabilitation
medicalTreatments.description: Essential long-term management for Korsakoff syndrome. Continued drinking prevents any further thiamine benefit and accelerates further hippocampal atrophy. Supervised alcohol detoxification, thiamine continuation, and high-protein nutrition support partial recovery.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: At 2-year follow-up, approximately 25% of Korsakoff patients who achieve and maintain abstinence show clinically meaningful memory improvement (Kopelman 2002)

alternativeTreatments.name: Spaced retrieval training with digital reminders
alternativeTreatments.description: Evidence-based behavioural technique where patients practice recalling specific personally relevant information at systematically expanding intervals using smartphone alarms or specialized memory apps. A 2019 meta-analysis (PMID 31072671) found moderate effect sizes for functional independence in amnestic populations.

whySeeSpecialist: Neurologists confirm the diagnosis, identify the causal condition, and exclude treatable causes before labelling the amnesia as permanent. Neuropsychologists perform the formal memory characterization needed for accurate prognosis, rehabilitation planning, and capacity assessments. Addiction medicine physicians are essential co-managers when alcohol use disorder underlies the syndrome.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a neurologist experienced in cognitive disorders who has access to neuropsychological testing and MRI with hippocampal protocols. For Korsakoff syndrome, a service linking neurology with addiction medicine and neurorehabilitation provides the best outcomes. Confirm the centre performs formal neuropsychological assessment, not just bedside cognitive screening.
hospitalCriteria: Access to IV thiamine and ability to recognize and treat Wernicke encephalopathy as a medical emergency
Neuropsychology service capable of formal memory battery assessment
MRI with coronal hippocampal sequences and FLAIR
Inpatient neurorehabilitation unit for severe post-traumatic or post-encephalitic cases
Liaison psychiatry and addiction medicine services for alcohol-related cases
keyFacilities: Addiction medicine services with medical detoxification capability
preventionStrategies: Treat alcohol use disorder early with supervised detoxification, naltrexone or acamprosate pharmacotherapy, and ensure oral thiamine supplementation for all patients with heavy alcohol use.
Give empirical IV thiamine to any malnourished or alcohol-dependent patient presenting to the emergency department before glucose administration.
Supplement thiamine routinely in all patients undergoing bariatric surgery, prolonged fasting, or total parenteral nutrition lacking B vitamins.
Reduce road-traffic and fall risk through seatbelt use, fall-prevention programs in older adults, and effective headwear in sports.
Use acyclovir promptly in any febrile encephalopathic patient with temporal-lobe MRI changes while awaiting HSV PCR results.
Optimise bystander CPR quality and speed to minimise no-flow time after cardiac arrest, reducing hippocampal anoxic injury.
lifestyleModifications: Maintain complete alcohol abstinence following Korsakoff syndrome diagnosis — continued drinking negates any chance of recovery and accelerates further hippocampal atrophy.
Use external memory aids systematically: a single structured notebook, smartphone reminders, and a visible calendar reduce functional disability independently of any intrinsic memory recovery.
Establish a fixed daily routine — predictable sequences reduce reliance on intact memory and limit disorientation.
Engage regularly with occupational therapy to adapt the living environment with labelled storage, reminders on doors, and simplified medication systems.
Maintain adequate thiamine and B-vitamin intake through dietary diversity (whole grains, legumes, lean meat) and supplementation as prescribed.
Participate in structured social and cognitive activity; even dense amnesia spares procedural learning and social skills which can be leveraged to maintain quality of life.

recommended: Thiamine-rich foods: whole grains, legumes, pork, and seeds — to maintain B1 status after repletion
Folate-containing foods (leafy greens, fortified cereals) to support ongoing neuronal function
Adequate protein (1-1.2 g/kg/day) during nutritional rehabilitation in malnourished patients
Omega-3 fatty acids from oily fish — early evidence suggests neuroprotective role after TBI (PMID 22546688)
avoid: Alcohol in any amount following Korsakoff or post-encephalitic amnesia diagnosis
Skipping meals or prolonged fasting, which can precipitate thiamine depletion recurrence
Raw fish (thiaminase in some species can degrade dietary thiamine)
High-sugar diets without concurrent thiamine supplementation, particularly in recovery from Wernicke episodes

exerciseGuidelines: Aerobic exercise 30 minutes at least 5 days per week is encouraged; exercise promotes BDNF-mediated hippocampal neurogenesis in animal models and improves mood, sleep, and functional capacity in TBI rehabilitation trials. Contact and high-risk sports should be avoided for at least 6 months after TBI. Ataxia in Korsakoff syndrome may require balance-focused physiotherapy before unsupervised exercise.
dailyManagement: Check the day's schedule from a written planner or phone calendar first thing each morning to orient to current date and planned activities.
Keep a memory notebook or digital app for new names, appointments, and instructions — review it before and after every significant interaction.
Take prescribed thiamine and vitamin B complex at the same time each day, using a blister pack or pill organiser to confirm compliance.
Attend scheduled follow-up with neurology or memory clinic; bring a caregiver or family member who can provide collateral history.
Avoid situations that require reliable prospective memory (driving, complex financial decisions, medication self-management) until formal neuropsychological assessment has determined capacity.
prognosis: Prognosis depends heavily on cause and speed of treatment. In Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome, studies consistently report that only 20-25% of patients show meaningful long-term memory recovery; the remaining 75-80% have a permanent moderate-to-severe amnestic syndrome. Recovery is more likely in patients under 60, those achieving sustained alcohol abstinence, and those given IV thiamine promptly. Post-traumatic amnesia lasting under 24 hours typically resolves fully; lasting over 4 weeks predicts permanent moderate-to-severe memory impairment. Post-herpetic amnesia — even with prompt acyclovir — leaves roughly 50-60% of survivors with clinically significant memory deficits at 12 months. Post-anoxic hippocampal amnesia after cardiac arrest has an intermediate prognosis depending on downtime: intact anterograde memory recovery occurs in 30-40% of cases with short no-flow times. Functional independence with rehabilitation is achievable even in dense amnesia through procedural learning and environmental adaptation strategies.
recoveryTimeline: Acute Wernicke encephalopathy responds to IV thiamine within 24-48 hours (ocular signs resolve first); memory improvement, if it occurs, emerges over weeks to months and plateaus by 12-24 months. Post-traumatic amnesia duration directly predicts recovery trajectory. Post-encephalitic amnesia tends to plateau by 12-18 months. Functional gains from neuropsychological rehabilitation continue for 2-3 years after injury regardless of structural recovery.
complications: Permanent severe anterograde amnesia preventing independent living — occurs in 75-80% of untreated Korsakoff syndrome and a significant proportion of post-encephalitic cases; requires long-term supported accommodation.
Falls and accidents due to temporal disorientation and impaired prospective memory — a leading cause of hospitalisation in amnestic patients living in the community.
Depression and anxiety, present in up to 40% of patients with amnestic disorder, exacerbated by awareness of memory loss; treat with antidepressants and psychological support.
Alcohol relapse in Korsakoff syndrome, negating thiamine benefit and causing further hippocampal damage — relapse rates exceed 50% without structured addiction support.

Capacity and safeguarding issues — patients with severe amnesia may lack the capacity to consent to medical treatment, manage finances, or live safely without supervision; formal capacity assessment is mandatory.

Caregiver burnout — the repetitive questioning, confabulation, and 24-hour supervision required in severe amnestic disorder places extreme demands on families; structured respite and carer support are essential.

supportResources.name: Alzheimer's Society UK — Amnesia and Memory Loss Information
supportResources.description: Plain-language guidance on alcohol-related brain damage including Korsakoff syndrome, with support pathways.

supportResources.name: Brain Injury Association of America
supportResources.description: Resources for TBI survivors including memory rehabilitation, caregiver guides, and state-level support services.

supportResources.name: NHS Inform — Wernicke-Korsakoff Syndrome
supportResources.description: UK-based clinical overview of Korsakoff syndrome with referral pathways and practical management advice.

confusedWithConditions.name: Alzheimer's disease
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Alzheimer's disease is a progressive neurodegenerative dementia producing global cognitive decline across memory, language, executive function, and visuospatial domains. Amnestic disorder due to physiological causes produces isolated or predominant memory loss with preserved general intelligence and no progressive cortical spread. Alzheimer's lacks a discrete precipitating event, while F04 requires one.

confusedWithConditions.name: Dissociative amnesia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Dissociative amnesia (F44.0) results from psychological trauma without identifiable structural brain pathology; MRI and neuropsychological profiles differ from organic amnesia. Retrograde amnesia is often selective, dense, and patchy — unlike the temporal gradient of Korsakoff — and can recover suddenly with psychotherapy. No MRI lesion or metabolic cause is found.

confusedWithConditions.name: Transient global amnesia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Transient global amnesia resolves completely within 24 hours, leaving no residual memory deficit, and is distinguished by its abrupt onset during physical or emotional stress in middle-aged or older adults. Amnestic disorder F04 is persistent by definition; the acute presentation may mimic TGA but the absence of recovery identifies the chronic form.

confusedWithConditions.name: Depression with pseudodementia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Severe depression can produce subjective memory complaints and poor performance on cognitive tests due to impaired effort and attention rather than structural hippocampal damage. Unlike F04, cognitive symptoms resolve with antidepressant treatment, MRI is normal, and patients typically show full recall when effort is controlled for during testing.

relatedConditions.name: Alzheimer's Disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Both produce anterograde amnesia as a core feature; differentiating isolated amnestic disorder from early Alzheimer's drives specialist referral and treatment decisions.

relatedConditions.name: Stroke
relatedConditions.relevance: Posterior circulation strokes causing hippocampal or thalamic infarction are a common vascular cause of organic amnestic disorder.

relatedConditions.name: Epilepsy
relatedConditions.relevance: Complex partial seizures can mimic amnestic episodes, and chronic temporal-lobe epilepsy causes progressive hippocampal sclerosis with lasting memory deficits.

relatedConditions.name: Multiple Sclerosis
relatedConditions.relevance: MS plaques in the fornix or medial temporal structures can produce amnestic syndromes indistinguishable from other organic causes, and memory impairment affects 40-60% of people with MS.

faqs.question: What is amnestic disorder due to a physiological condition?
faqs.answer: It is a persistent memory impairment directly caused by an identifiable medical event — such as thiamine deficiency, brain injury, herpes encephalitis, or cardiac arrest — that damages the brain circuits that form and store memories. Unlike dissociative amnesia, a physical brain cause is confirmed. It is coded ICD-10 F04.

faqs.question: What is the most common cause of amnestic disorder?
faqs.answer: Thiamine deficiency from chronic alcohol use disorder is the leading cause, producing Korsakoff syndrome. It damages the mammillary bodies and medial thalamus. Traumatic brain injury, herpes encephalitis, and hypoxic brain injury after cardiac arrest are the next most common causes.

faqs.question: Can amnestic disorder be cured?
faqs.answer: Cure depends on the cause. Promptly treated Wernicke encephalopathy with IV thiamine prevents permanent Korsakoff syndrome in roughly 80% of cases. Established Korsakoff syndrome is permanent in about 75% of patients. Post-traumatic and post-encephalitic amnesia shows variable partial recovery over 12-24 months.

faqs.question: What is the difference between anterograde and retrograde amnesia?
faqs.answer: Anterograde amnesia is the inability to form new memories after the brain injury — patients cannot recall what they ate for lunch or names they were just told. Retrograde amnesia is loss of memories from before the injury. Most physiological amnestic disorders produce more anterograde than retrograde loss.

faqs.question: What is Korsakoff syndrome?
faqs.answer: Korsakoff syndrome is a permanent amnestic disorder caused by thiamine deficiency, most often from chronic alcohol use. It produces dense anterograde amnesia, retrograde memory loss spanning years, confabulation, and temporal disorientation. It almost always follows an acute Wernicke encephalopathy episode that was not adequately treated with IV thiamine.

faqs.question: How is amnestic disorder diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis requires a neuropsychological memory battery to document the pattern of impairment, MRI brain to identify structural cause (mammillary body atrophy, hippocampal signal change, infarcts), and relevant blood tests or CSF analysis to identify the physiological cause. Bedside screening tests like the MoCA are not sufficient alone.

faqs.question: What is confabulation in amnestic disorder?
faqs.answer: Confabulation is the production of false memories without intention to deceive. Patients with Korsakoff syndrome fill gaps in memory with detailed but inaccurate stories about their recent activities or whereabouts. It differs from lying in that patients genuinely believe their fabricated accounts and cannot self-correct when challenged.

faqs.question: How is Korsakoff syndrome treated?
faqs.answer: Immediate treatment is intravenous thiamine 500 mg three times daily for at least 3-5 days, given before any glucose. Oral thiamine is continued long-term. Alcohol abstinence is essential. Neuropsychological rehabilitation using memory books, structured routines, and errorless learning improves functional independence even when core amnesia persists.

faqs.question: Does amnesia from brain injury get better over time?
faqs.answer: Post-traumatic amnesia typically improves most rapidly in the first 6 months. Post-traumatic amnesia lasting under 24 hours usually resolves; lasting over 4 weeks predicts significant permanent deficit. Recovery plateaus by 12-24 months in most cases. Structured rehabilitation continues to improve functional skills even after biological recovery has plateaued.

faqs.question: What is the difference between amnestic disorder and dementia?
faqs.answer: Amnestic disorder F04 involves isolated or predominant memory impairment with relatively preserved intelligence, language, and executive function, caused by a specific brain insult. Dementia involves progressive global cognitive decline across multiple domains. Amnestic disorder does not typically progress beyond the initial insult if the cause is treated.

faqs.question: Is thiamine given before glucose in a malnourished patient?
faqs.answer: Yes. Glucose administration in a thiamine-depleted patient can precipitate acute Wernicke encephalopathy by consuming the last available thiamine stores. IV thiamine must be administered before or simultaneously with any glucose-containing fluid in any malnourished, alcohol-dependent, or at-risk patient presenting to hospital.

faqs.question: Can herpes encephalitis cause permanent amnesia?
faqs.answer: Yes. HSV-1 encephalitis destroys temporal-lobe and hippocampal tissue, and 50-70% of survivors retain significant anterograde amnesia even after adequate acyclovir treatment. Earlier treatment correlates with better memory outcomes. Recovery plateaus by 12-18 months post-infection.

faqs.question: How is memory rehabilitation done for amnestic disorder?
faqs.answer: Rehabilitation uses errorless learning (practising tasks without making errors so procedural memory reinforces correct responses), spaced retrieval (practising recall at increasing intervals), and external memory aids such as notebooks, smartphones, and structured daily schedules. These approaches improve daily functioning even in patients with dense anterograde amnesia.

faqs.question: Can people with amnestic disorder live independently?
faqs.answer: Independence depends on severity. Mild amnestic disorder with effective memory aids may allow safe independent living. Severe Korsakoff syndrome typically requires supported accommodation or residential care due to inability to manage medications, finances, or meals safely. Formal capacity assessment determines level of support needed.

faqs.question: Does alcohol abstinence improve Korsakoff syndrome?
faqs.answer: Yes, but only modestly. About 25% of patients who achieve sustained abstinence show clinically meaningful memory improvement at 2 years. Continued drinking prevents any recovery and accelerates further hippocampal damage. Abstinence is the single most important modifiable factor after thiamine repletion.

metaDescription: Amnestic disorder: persistent memory loss from brain injury, thiamine deficiency, or encephalitis. Korsakoff syndrome, diagnosis and treatment reviewed.
